title: Which countries include mumps vaccines in their national vaccination programs?
subtitle: This shows which countries provide and recommend mumps vaccines through routine services. People may still be able to receive the vaccine if it's not in the routine schedule – it might be optional or available commercially.
caption:
Which countries include mumps vaccines in their national vaccination programs?

This shows which countries provide and recommend mumps vaccines through routine services. People may still be able to receive the vaccine if it's not in the routine schedule – it might be optional or available commercially.

The choropleth map displays the status of mumps vaccination within national immunization schedules across the globe for the year 2024, categorizing nations based on whether the vaccine is administered routinely across the entire country, not routinely administered, or administered only in specific regions. The visualization utilizes a color scale where teal indicates routine administration across the entire country, salmon pink denotes areas where the vaccine is not routinely administered, and orange represents administration in only regions of the country, while grey hatching marks areas with no available data. A large portion of the Americas, including the United States, Canada, Brazil, and Argentina, along with most of Europe, Russia, China, and Australia, falls into the teal category, showing widespread routine inclusion in these territories. Conversely, the salmon pink color dominates the African continent, covering nations such as Nigeria, Ethiopia, and the Democratic Republic of the Congo, as well as extending across South Asia to include India, Pakistan, and Bangladesh, and parts of Southeast Asia like Indonesia. Japan and South Korea appear in the orange band, signifying that the vaccine is included in the schedule for only regions of the country rather than the nation as a whole. Greenland is marked with grey hatching to indicate missing data. The geographic distribution presents a clear divide between the Global North and parts of South America, where routine national programs are standard, and the Global South, particularly Africa and South Asia, where routine administration is less common. Improved mumps vaccines became available for the first time in 1967, yet the map shows that routine integration into national programs remains uneven across different world regions more than fifty years later. The most visible pattern is the concentration of non-routine administration across the equatorial belt and South Asia, contrasting with the near-universal routine coverage in North America and Europe.
